note: Cornerstone MTSS assessment library. Fluency passages are original compositions aligned in format and Lexile range to DIBELS/mClass ORF. Research citations are provided per tool. NOT a reprint of any commercial assessment.

researchBasis.orfNorms: Hasbrouck, J. & Tindal, G. (2017). An update to compiled ORF norms (Technical Report No. 1702). Behavioral Research and Teaching, University of Oregon.
researchBasis.dibelsPA: Good, R.H. & Kaminski, R.A. (2002). DIBELS: Dynamic Indicators of Basic Early Literacy Skills. Institute for Development of Educational Achievement.
researchBasis.numberTalks: Harris, P.W. (2011). Building Powerful Numeracy for Middle and High School Students. Heinemann. / Parrish, S. (2014). Number Talks. Math Solutions.
researchBasis.phonicsPacing: Mesmer, H.A.E. & Griffith, P.L. (2005). Everybody's reading the law — but what does it really say about phonics instruction? The Reading Teacher, 59(4), 366–376.

fluencyPassages.title: The Red Hat
fluencyPassages.text: I have a hat. My hat is red. I put my hat on. It fits! The hat is big. Dad has a hat too. His hat is blue. We like our hats. We go for a walk. I see a dog. The dog runs. We run too. What fun we had.
fluencyPassages.teacherNotes: Read the full passage once as a model before student reads. For fall Kindergarten, this is a listening comprehension task — student may track with finger. Count words read correctly in 60 seconds.
fluencyPassages.scoringGuide: Count words read correctly per minute (WCPM). Omissions, substitutions, and hesitations over 3 seconds count as errors. Insertions do not count against score.

fluencyPassages.title: The Big Splash
fluencyPassages.text: Sam and Pip went to the pond. The sun was hot. Pip jumped in with a big splash! Sam got wet. Sam was mad. But then Sam felt the cool water on his feet. Sam jumped in too! They splashed and splashed. Mom called them for lunch. They came out dripping wet. Mom gave them big towels. Sam and Pip smiled at each other. It was a great day.
fluencyPassages.teacherNotes: Student reads independently. Begin timing when student reads first word. If student struggles after 3 seconds on a word, say the word and mark as error.
fluencyPassages.scoringGuide: Count words read correctly per minute (WCPM). Spring K benchmark: 20 WCPM (50th percentile per Hasbrouck & Tindal 2017).

fluencyPassages.title: The Cat and the Nap
fluencyPassages.text: The cat sat on the mat. She had a nap. The dog came in. The dog was big. The cat woke up fast. She ran to the top of the steps. The dog looked up at her. He wagged his tail. The cat did not come down. She sat and looked at the dog. After a long time, the dog went out. The cat came back down. She sat on the mat. She had another nap.
fluencyPassages.teacherNotes: Grade 1 fall passage uses short vowels and CVC patterns only. Matches phonics sequence through Lesson 15-20 of UFLI or Fundations Level 1.
fluencyPassages.scoringGuide: Grade 1 fall benchmark (50th %ile): 23 WCPM. Below 10 WCPM = significant concern for ORF development.

fluencyPassages.title: The Bike Ride
fluencyPassages.text: Jake had a new bike. It was bright red with silver wheels. He had been waiting all week to ride it. On Saturday morning, he woke up early. The sun was already shining. Jake ate his breakfast quickly and put on his helmet. He walked his bike to the end of the driveway. He put one foot on the pedal and pushed off. For a second, he wobbled. Then he was riding! He went down the hill and around the bend. He passed three houses and turned at the big oak tree. When he got home, he was out of breath and smiling. This was the best Saturday ever.
fluencyPassages.teacherNotes: Spring Grade 1 passage includes CVCe, digraphs, and blends. Matches phonics through Lesson 80-90 of UFLI or Fundations Level 1 Unit 6.
fluencyPassages.scoringGuide: Grade 1 spring benchmark (50th %ile): 71 WCPM. 53 WCPM = winter benchmark. Record errors and calculate WCPM.

fluencyPassages.title: The Science Fair
fluencyPassages.text: Every year, the school holds a science fair. Students pick a topic, do an experiment, and share what they learn. This year, Maya wanted to find out which type of soil helps plants grow best. She planted bean seeds in three different pots: one with clay soil, one with sandy soil, and one with potting mix. She watered each pot the same amount every day. She measured the plants every Monday. After six weeks, the plant in the potting mix was tallest. The clay soil plant grew only a little bit. The sandy soil plant fell over from lack of water. Maya made a chart and wrote up her results. The judges gave her a blue ribbon for best experiment in her grade.
fluencyPassages.teacherNotes: Grade 2 fall passage includes multisyllabic words, vowel teams, and r-controlled vowels. If student misreads a word with an advanced pattern, note the error type for phonics planning.
fluencyPassages.scoringGuide: Grade 2 fall benchmark (50th %ile): 72 WCPM. Record error types to inform targeted phonics instruction.

fluencyPassages.title: The Coral Reef
fluencyPassages.text: Coral reefs are one of the most amazing places on Earth. They are found in warm, shallow ocean water, mostly near the equator. Even though coral looks like colorful rocks or plants, it is actually made up of tiny animals called polyps. Each polyp builds a hard shell around its body. Over hundreds of years, these shells pile up and form the reef structure we see today. Coral reefs are home to more than one quarter of all ocean species. Fish, sea turtles, octopuses, and thousands of other creatures live in and around reefs. Scientists call coral reefs the rainforests of the sea because so many species depend on them. Today, coral reefs are in danger from warming water, pollution, and overfishing. Protecting reefs means protecting thousands of species at once.
fluencyPassages.teacherNotes: Grade 2 spring passage includes content-area vocabulary (equator, polyps, species). Pre-teach 2-3 key words if assessing comprehension alongside fluency.
fluencyPassages.scoringGuide: Grade 2 spring benchmark (50th %ile): 100 WCPM. Note phrasing and prosody in addition to accuracy.

fluencyPassages.title: The Market Garden
fluencyPassages.text: On the edge of town, there was a small community garden that everyone called the Market Garden. Families rented plots of land for fifteen dollars a season. They could grow whatever they wanted — tomatoes, squash, herbs, flowers, or anything else they had seeds for. Every Saturday morning, gardeners brought their extra produce to a table near the entrance and left it out for neighbors to take for free. Mrs. Okafor always left baskets of her famous heirloom tomatoes, which were deep red and tasted nothing like the ones from the grocery store. Mr. Patel grew rows and rows of hot peppers, which he dried and ground into powder. The Martinez family planted corn that grew so tall it blocked the path by August. The Market Garden was not just about vegetables. It was about neighbors becoming friends.
fluencyPassages.teacherNotes: Grade 3 passage includes multi-syllabic words and complex syntax. This level is appropriate for on-grade-level monitoring. Use with students reading at 3rd grade level.
fluencyPassages.scoringGuide: Grade 3 fall benchmark (50th %ile): 93 WCPM. Below 70 WCPM in fall = consider fluency intervention.

fluencyPassages.title: Hummingbirds
fluencyPassages.text: Hummingbirds are among the smallest birds in the world, yet they are also some of the most remarkable. These tiny birds can beat their wings up to eighty times per second, which creates the humming sound that gives them their name. Because they use so much energy flying, hummingbirds must eat constantly — visiting hundreds of flowers each day to drink nectar with their long, tube-shaped tongues. A hummingbird's heart beats more than one thousand times per minute when it is active. To survive cold nights, a hummingbird can enter a state called torpor, where its heart rate and body temperature drop dramatically, almost like a brief hibernation. Despite their small size, hummingbirds are fierce. They will chase away birds many times their own size that try to steal their food sources. Only two things can threaten a hummingbird: a very cold snap that destroys flowers, or the loss of habitat when forests are cleared.
fluencyPassages.teacherNotes: Spring Grade 3 benchmark passage. Content-area vocabulary (nectar, torpor, habitat) enriches vocabulary monitoring alongside fluency.
fluencyPassages.scoringGuide: Grade 3 spring benchmark (50th %ile): 118 WCPM. 107 WCPM = winter benchmark.

fluencyPassages.title: The Pony Express
fluencyPassages.text: In 1860, before telephones or the internet, getting a message from Missouri to California took weeks by stagecoach. A group of businessmen had a bold idea: a relay system using fast horses and young riders who could carry mail across nearly two thousand miles in just ten days. They called it the Pony Express. Riders were required to weigh less than 125 pounds, and they were expected to ride hard through all kinds of weather, day and night. About 190 stations, placed roughly ten to fifteen miles apart, provided fresh horses so the mail never stopped moving. Riders switched horses in about two minutes and then galloped on. The most famous moment in Pony Express history came when riders carried President Lincoln's inaugural address to California in just seven days and seventeen hours. Despite its legendary status, the Pony Express only operated for nineteen months. When the transcontinental telegraph was completed in October 1861, the need for riders disappeared almost overnight.
fluencyPassages.teacherNotes: Grade 4 passage includes historical content, complex sentences, and Tier 3 vocabulary (inaugural, transcontinental). Good for both fluency and comprehension monitoring.
fluencyPassages.scoringGuide: Grade 4 fall benchmark (50th %ile): 110 WCPM. Below 85 WCPM at fall Grade 4 = consider intensive fluency support.

fluencyPassages.title: Ocean Currents
fluencyPassages.text: The oceans of the world are not still bodies of water. They are constantly in motion, driven by forces that include wind, temperature differences, and the rotation of the Earth. These movements are called ocean currents, and they act like giant conveyor belts that move water — and heat — from one part of the planet to another. Near the surface, wind is the main driver. Trade winds push water across the equator, where it is heated by the sun, and carry it toward the poles. In the deep ocean, differences in temperature and saltiness (called salinity) cause water to sink or rise in a process known as thermohaline circulation. The Gulf Stream is one of the most well-known surface currents. It carries warm water from the Gulf of Mexico up the East Coast of North America and across the Atlantic toward Northern Europe. Without the Gulf Stream, countries like Norway and the United Kingdom would be far colder than they are today. Scientists monitor ocean currents carefully, because changes in these currents can alter weather patterns and affect fisheries worldwide.
fluencyPassages.teacherNotes: Grade 4 spring science passage. Pair fluency score with a 2-question comprehension check for deeper monitoring data.

fluencyPassages.title: Rebuilding Notre-Dame
fluencyPassages.text: On the evening of April 15, 2019, a fire broke out in the roof of Notre-Dame Cathedral in Paris, France. Within hours, the famous spire had collapsed, and much of the roof was destroyed. People around the world watched in shock as the 856-year-old cathedral burned. Notre-Dame had survived the French Revolution, two World Wars, and centuries of change. Now it faced a new kind of crisis. Within days, donations poured in from governments, corporations, and individuals — eventually totaling more than one billion euros. The rebuilding effort became one of the most ambitious restoration projects in modern history. Thousands of artisans, architects, and historians joined forces to reconstruct the cathedral using techniques as close to the original medieval methods as possible. Craftspeople relearned forgotten skills — how to cut stone by hand, how to carve oak beams with traditional tools, how to apply lead roofing the way builders did in the 1100s. The project also used modern technology: laser scans made before the fire provided an exact digital blueprint of every stone and carving. French officials announced that Notre-Dame would reopen to visitors in December 2024.
fluencyPassages.teacherNotes: Grade 5 passage with sophisticated vocabulary and complex sentence structures. Appropriate for monitoring on-grade-level readers.

fluencyPassages.title: The Turing Test
fluencyPassages.text: In 1950, a British mathematician named Alan Turing posed a question that would shape the future of technology: Can machines think? To explore this question, he designed a thought experiment now known as the Turing Test. In the test, a human evaluator has a text-based conversation with two participants — one human and one computer. If the evaluator cannot reliably tell which is which, the computer is said to have passed the test. Turing did not claim that passing the test meant a machine was conscious or sentient. He was simply proposing a practical measure: if a machine behaves indistinguishably from a human in conversation, we can say it has achieved some form of intelligence. For decades, the Turing Test remained more of a philosophical concept than a practical benchmark. But as artificial intelligence programs became more sophisticated, researchers began designing chatbots specifically trained to fool human judges. In 2014, a program called Eugene Goostman was reported by some to have passed the test. Experts debated whether the result was genuine or the product of clever tricks. Today, as AI-generated text becomes harder to distinguish from human writing, Turing's question feels more relevant than ever.
fluencyPassages.teacherNotes: Grade 5 spring passage. High-interest STEM topic with abstract concepts. Consider pairing with a retell or 3-question comprehension probe.
fluencyPassages.scoringGuide: Grade 5 spring benchmark (50th %ile): 143 WCPM. 133 = winter benchmark. Students reading below 100 WCPM in spring Grade 5 should receive intensive fluency intervention.

numberTalks.title: How Many Dots?
numberTalks.problem: Show a domino with 2 dots on one side and 3 dots on the other. Ask: How many dots do you see? How do you know?
numberTalks.teacherScript: Show the domino face for 3 seconds, then cover it. 'How many dots did you see? Put a thumb up when you have an answer — no calling out yet.' After most have thumbs up: 'Tell me what you saw.' Record all strategies on the board without judgment. 'Did anyone see it a different way?'
numberTalks.teacherNotes: The goal is NOT to evaluate — it's to surface strategies. Honor 'I counted all' before moving to more efficient strategies. Never call 'counting all' slow or wrong.

numberTalks.title: Counting On from the Larger Number
numberTalks.problem: Show 5 dots grouped clearly, then 2 more separately. Ask: How many total? How did you count?
anticipatedStrategies: Starting from 5 and counting on 2 more
numberTalks.teacherScript: After showing the image: 'I'm going to record how we counted. Watch.' Write student's counting path on the board. If a student says 'I started at 5 and counted on', circle the 5 to show it was held in mind. 'So instead of starting at 1, we can start at the biggest number. Let's try together.'
numberTalks.teacherNotes: This is a bridge talk — moves students from counting all to counting on. Expect this to take 2-3 sessions before most students consistently count on.

numberTalks.title: Full Ten Frame
numberTalks.problem: Show a full ten frame (10 dots). Then show 10 and 1 more. Ask: How many now?
anticipatedStrategies: Seeing the full ten frame as 10 automatically
numberTalks.teacherScript: 'This frame has how many dots?' (10). 'Now I add one more — where does it go? How many do we have now?' Record: 10 + 1 = 11. 'What if I add one more? And one more?' Build the teen number sequence through 15.
numberTalks.teacherNotes: Ten frames anchor place value understanding. Students who subitize the full frame as '10' are developing essential numeracy — celebrate this explicitly.

numberTalks.title: Making Ten Strategy
numberTalks.problem: Write on board: 8 + 5. Ask: What's 8 + 5? How did you figure it out?
numberTalks.teacherScript: 'Take a moment — no hands yet. Think about 8 + 5.' (Pause 20-30 seconds) 'Thumbs when ready.' Call on 2-3 students. Record each method on the board with arrows showing the thinking. 'Maria started at 8 and counted on. Let me draw that on the number line. David used the ten — let me show that with a jump to 10 then a jump of 3.'
numberTalks.teacherNotes: Making ten is a key Grade 1 strategy. Students who only count on are still developing. Make ten visible with an open number line drawing — don't just write the equation.

numberTalks.title: Doubles and Near Doubles
numberTalks.problem: Write: 6 + 6. Then: 6 + 7. Ask: How are these related?
numberTalks.teacherScript: 'Let's do 6 + 6 first. Who knows this one?' (Many hands) '12! Great. Now — 6 + 7. Don't count — can you use what you know about 6 + 6?' Wait. 'How is 6 + 7 related to 6 + 6?' Record both equations side by side. Draw an arrow and write +1.
numberTalks.teacherNotes: Near doubles is one of Pam Harris's foundational 'helper facts.' The key move is making the relationship explicit on paper so students see the addend increased by 1.

numberTalks.title: Bridging to 20
numberTalks.problem: Write: 9 + 6. Ask students to solve it and explain using an open number line.
anticipatedStrategies: 9+6 → double 8 doesn't apply here → making ten is most natural
numberTalks.teacherScript: Give students mini whiteboards with a blank line drawn. 'Show me how you'd jump from 9 to get to the answer.' Circulate and select 3 different-level responses to share. Sequence from least to most efficient: count-on → make-ten.
numberTalks.teacherNotes: The five practices (anticipate, monitor, select, sequence, connect) work perfectly here. Plan which student strategies to share BEFORE the number talk begins.

numberTalks.title: Two-Digit Addition — Jump Strategy
numberTalks.problem: Write: 47 + 25. Ask: How could you add these without the standard algorithm?
numberTalks.teacherScript: '47 + 25. No pencil-paper algorithm — use your number sense.' (Pause) After sharing strategies, record all three on open number lines side by side. 'Which strategy do you think is most efficient? Why?'
numberTalks.teacherNotes: Grade 2 is where mental addition of 2-digit numbers becomes a focus. Students who only use the standard algorithm have not developed number sense — the number talk surfaces this.

numberTalks.title: Subtraction — Constant Difference
numberTalks.problem: Write: 52 - 19. Ask: Is there a way to make this easier before you subtract?
anticipatedStrategies: Standard algorithm (expected — name it, then offer the relationship strategy)
numberTalks.teacherScript: '52 minus 19. Before you compute — look at the 19. What if we changed 19 to 20? What would we have to do to 52 to keep the difference the same?' Pause for discovery. 'If we add 1 to both numbers, the gap stays the same. So 53-20=33.'
numberTalks.teacherNotes: Constant difference is one of Pam Harris's high-leverage subtraction strategies. Many students have never seen subtraction as distance on a number line — this unlocks it.

numberTalks.title: Skip Counting by 5s and 10s
numberTalks.problem: Start at 35. Count forward by 5s to 75. Then backward by 10s from 95 to 15.
anticipatedStrategies: Using patterns in the ones digit
numberTalks.teacherScript: Do this as a whole class choral count. Point to numbers on a number line as class counts. Then: 'What patterns do you notice? What happens to the tens digit? The ones digit?'
numberTalks.teacherNotes: Choral counting (Kazemi & Hintz) is a variant of number talks that builds patterning and place value understanding. Record the count on the board and let students discover the patterns.

numberTalks.title: Multiplication — Partial Products
numberTalks.problem: Write: 6 × 14. Ask: Can you break this apart to make it easier?
numberTalks.teacherScript: Draw an open array (rectangle) 6 units tall and 14 units wide on the board. 'Where could I cut this rectangle to make two easier problems?' Students suggest splitting 14 into 10+4. Fill in the partial products.
numberTalks.teacherNotes: The area model makes multiplication visual and connects to the distributive property. Grade 3 students benefit enormously from seeing multiplication as area before learning the algorithm.

numberTalks.title: Doubling and Halving
numberTalks.problem: Write: 4 × 18. Then: 2 × 36. Then: 8 × 9. Ask: Are these the same? How do you know?
anticipatedStrategies: Seeing that doubling one factor while halving the other keeps the product the same
numberTalks.teacherScript: 'Compute each one. (Pause) Now look at all three answers. What do you notice?' Record all three side by side. Draw arrows showing the doubling/halving relationship.
numberTalks.teacherNotes: Doubling and halving is a high-leverage strategy that connects multiplication, division, and proportional reasoning. Most grade 3 students have never seen this relationship made explicit.

numberTalks.title: What Is One Third?
numberTalks.problem: Draw a rectangle. 'If this whole rectangle is 1, shade one-third. Now shade two-thirds. What do you notice about two-thirds and one-third?'
numberTalks.teacherScript: Have students draw on whiteboards. Select 3 different representations to discuss. 'Is a circle also okay? A number line? Let's show all three models for 1/3.'
numberTalks.teacherNotes: Unit fractions are the building block of all fraction work. Students who partition incorrectly (unequal parts) need concrete manipulatives before this number talk.

numberTalks.title: Multi-Digit Multiplication — Ratio Table
numberTalks.problem: Write: 23 × 15. Ask students to build a ratio table starting from what they know.
anticipatedStrategies: Standard algorithm (accept but ask for a second method)
numberTalks.teacherScript: Draw an empty ratio table with one column header: '×23'. Fill in rows together: 1, 2, 5, 10, then ask 'How can we use these to get 15?' Students suggest 10+5=15, so add 230+115=345.
numberTalks.teacherNotes: Ratio tables are one of Pam Harris's most versatile tools. They bridge skip counting (Grade 2-3) and proportional reasoning (Grade 6-7). Use them daily for 2-3 weeks to build fluency.

numberTalks.title: Equivalent Fractions on a Number Line
numberTalks.problem: Write: 1/2, 2/4, 4/8. Are these the same? Prove it three ways.
anticipatedStrategies: Multiplying numerator and denominator by the same number
Placing on a number line and landing at the same point
Shading equal-sized pieces on an area model
numberTalks.teacherScript: Draw a number line 0-1. 'Place all three fractions. What do you notice?' Then draw an area model. 'Shade each fraction. What happens to the shaded area as we increase the number of pieces?'
numberTalks.teacherNotes: Multiple representations of equivalence (number line, area model, and equation) are all required by common standards. Number talks are ideal for building all three simultaneously.

numberTalks.title: Multi-Digit Addition — Compensation Strategy
numberTalks.problem: Write: 398 + 247. Ask: Is there an easier problem nearby?
numberTalks.teacherScript: '398 is close to what friendly number?' (400) 'If I use 400 instead of 398, what do I need to fix at the end?' This makes the compensation explicit. Record on a number line.
numberTalks.teacherNotes: Compensation is a hallmark of flexible numeracy. Students who only have the standard algorithm find this difficult at first. Allow multiple practice sessions before expecting transfer.

numberTalks.title: Multiplying Fractions — Visual Model
numberTalks.problem: Write: 3/4 × 24. Ask: What does this mean? How could you represent it?
anticipatedStrategies: Drawing 24 objects and circling 3/4 of them
numberTalks.teacherScript: Draw a tape diagram of 24 divided into 4 equal sections. 'If this whole bar is 24, what is one-fourth? Three-fourths?' Connect the visual to the equation.
numberTalks.teacherNotes: Many Grade 5 students compute fraction × whole number mechanically without understanding. Tape diagrams make the meaning visible. Do this BEFORE introducing the fraction algorithm.

numberTalks.title: Decimal × 10 and ÷ 10
numberTalks.problem: Write: 3.7 × 10. Then: 3.7 ÷ 10. Ask: What changed? What stayed the same?
anticipatedStrategies: Using place value language: 3.7 → 37.0 when multiplied by 10
numberTalks.teacherScript: Use a place value chart (ones | tenths | hundredths). Move a counter for 3.7 one column left (×10) or right (÷10). 'The digit didn't change — its value did. Why?'
numberTalks.teacherNotes: The 'move the decimal point' shortcut is meaningless without place value understanding. Always ground this in a concrete chart or number line before teaching the shortcut.

numberTalks.title: Volume of a Rectangular Prism
numberTalks.problem: A box is 4 cm × 6 cm × 3 cm. How many unit cubes fit inside? How do you know?
numberTalks.teacherScript: Start with a physical model or drawing of one layer (4×6 array). 'How many cubes are in this layer? How many layers? How can we use multiplication to find the total without counting each cube?'
numberTalks.teacherNotes: Volume connects multiplication and spatial reasoning. Use connecting cubes or drawings. The goal is for students to see volume as repeated layers (multiplicative thinking).

screeners.administrationScript: Say: I am going to say a word. Tell me all the sounds you hear in the word. If I say [mat], you would say /m/ /ae/ /t/. Let's try one together. [Practice: cat] Ready? Let's begin. Read each word. After 3 seconds of no response, say Try the next one and mark as 0.
screeners.scoringGuide: Award 1 point per correct phoneme (not per word). Do not require phoneme notation — accept any representation that accurately identifies the sound. Total possible: 34 points.
screeners.researchCitation: Good, R.H. & Kaminski, R.A. (2002). DIBELS Phoneme Segmentation Fluency. University of Oregon.

screeners.administrationScript: Say: 'I am going to show you some make-believe words. Try to read each one the best you can. If you don't know a word, you can say the sounds. Ready?' Show card. Begin timing when student reads first letter sound. Record letter sounds correct (LSC) and whole words read (WRC) separately.
screeners.scoringGuide: Score letter-sound correspondences correct (LSC) per minute. If student reads the whole word correctly and fluently, count all sounds as correct. Mark errors: substitutions, omissions. Do not count self-corrections as errors.
screeners.researchCitation: Good, R.H. & Kaminski, R.A. (2002). DIBELS Nonsense Word Fluency. University of Oregon. / Torgesen, J.K. (2004). Preventing early reading failure.

screeners.administrationScript: Say: 'I am going to show you some words one at a time. Say each word as fast as you can. Ready?' Show each word card for 2 seconds. If student does not respond, move on. Mark incorrect responses.
screeners.scoringGuide: Score number of words read correctly in 3 minutes. Do not accept phonetic approximations — the response must be automatic and correct.
screeners.researchCitation: Dolch, E.W. (1948). Problems in reading. The Garrard Press. / Fry, E. (1980). The new instant word list. The Reading Teacher, 34(3), 284-289.

screeners.administrationScript: Say: 'I am going to read a short passage with you. Then I will ask you some questions. Listen carefully.' Read the passage aloud together if needed. Ask each question and record the student's response.
screeners.passageText: Long ago, most books were made by hand. Monks copied words onto animal skin called vellum. It took months to make one book. Then, in the 1400s, Johannes Gutenberg invented the printing press. Paper became important. Paper was made in large mills where workers turned wood pulp into sheets. Books became cheaper and more common. More people learned to read. Ideas spread faster than ever before.

items.question: What did monks write on before paper was common?
Why did books become cheaper after the printing press?
items.answer: Paper was faster/cheaper to make; more copies could be made

items.question: What is the main idea of this passage?
items.answer: How books became easier to make and spread ideas

screeners.scoringGuide: Score 1 point per correct response. Partial credit allowed for inferential questions if student demonstrates understanding of the relationship. 4/4 = benchmark. 2-3/4 = some risk. 0-1/4 = high risk.
screeners.researchCitation: Fuchs, L.S. & Fuchs, D. (1992). Identifying a measure for monitoring student reading progress. School Psychology Review, 21(1), 45-58.
